page.content: The analysis of the R-wave peak time at lead II across different age subgroups reveals significant variations, categorized under the header Subgroup (age), with male values and female values specified. For the age group 21 ~ 30, the male R-wave peak time is 28.98 ± 4.87 ms and the female R-wave peak time is 30.03 ± 3.54c, yielding a P value of 0.000. In the 31 ~ 40 age group, the male R-wave peak time is 29.67 ± 4.60 ms, while the female R-wave peak time is 30.75 ± 3.40b c, also with a P value of 0.000. The 41 ~ 50 age group shows a male R-wave peak time of 30.09 ± 5.08 ms and a female R-wave peak time of 29.35 ± 4.62, resulting in a P value of 0.181. For the 51 ~ 60 age group, the male R-wave peak time is 29.95 ± 4.91 ms and the female is 29.54 ± 4.95, with a P value of 0.502. In the 61 ~ 70 age group, the male R-wave peak time is 30.41 ± 4.80a ms and the female is 29.65 ± 5.16, leading to a P value of 0.453. Finally, the 71 ~ 80 age group displays a male R-wave peak time of 29.26 ± 5.63 ms and a female R-wave peak time of 28.95 ± 4.64c, with a P value of 0.005. The second table provides data on various organisms, with Drosophila melanogaster showing 265 matches and 12% of unique sequences, Mus musculus with 447 matches and 20%, Saccharomyces cerevisiae with 5 matches and 0.2%, and Caenorhabditis elegans with 113 matches and 5%. The total matches amount to 830, while the total unique sequences are 517, representing 23%.
